questionTextSource: Where must you wait?
explanationSource: A traffic light in operation has priority over traffic signs, which regulate the right of way. Also, if there is a stop line, stop there and wait until you are allowed to continue.
answerASource: At the stop line
answerBSource: At the point where you can see along the intersecting road (sight line)
answerCSource: Level with the traffic lights

questionTextSource: What is correct in this situation?
explanationSource: If a traffic light turns red, car drivers who want to drive straight ahead or to the left must stop and wait. If there is an additional traffic light with a glowing green arrow pointing to the right, it is allowed to continue driving and to turn right. A traffic light without additional traffic lights or arrows, however, is valid for all directions.
answerASource: Traffic turning right may proceed
answerBSource: Traffic continuing ahead may proceed
answerCSource: Traffic turning left may proceed

questionTextSource: What should you do?
explanationSource: The traffic light switches from green to yellow directly in front of you. Your speed is 45 km/h. So, you're too fast in order to slow down and stop in front of the traffic light. There is also a truck behind you, which could hit you while doing an abrupt braking maneuver. Drive on briskly.
answerASource: Continue driving
answerBSource: Slow down

questionTextSource: What is correct in this situation?
explanationSource: Basically, when turning left, you as a car driver have to grant the oncoming traffic right of way. Since in this situation, the green arrow lights on the opposite street corner, you can assume that the traffic light for the oncoming traffic is red. So, you can turn.
answerASource: I may turn unimpeded
answerBSource: I must allow the oncoming traffic to pass through
answerCSource: I have to wait in the centre of the junction

questionTextSource: You want to turn right at these traffic lights with a green arrow sign. What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Thanks to the green arrow sign, you can turn at this traffic light even when it's red. To do this, merge right and come to a brief stop in front of the traffic light. Afterwards, make sure that you do not obstruct or endanger other road users. Also note that cross traffic still has priority.
answerASource: I stop before the junction
answerBSource: I have to ensure that other road users are not impeded
answerCSource: I may turn right into any lane

questionTextSource: What should you do?
explanationSource: In front of you you see a traffic light changing from green to yellow. You drive at 40 km/h. Since you are still far enough from the traffic lights, you have to slow down.
answerASource: Slow down
answerBSource: Continue driving

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action now?
explanationSource: If the yellow traffic light flashes, the traffic light system is out of operation. Then the general right of way applies. You are not on a priority road; therefore, possible cross traffic has the right of way.
answerASource: I allow any cross traffic the right of way
answerBSource: I wait at the stop line for the next set of green lights
answerCSource: I drive through the junction quickly, because "yellow" is displayed

questionTextSource: Under what circumstances may you cross a junction during a red light?
explanationSource: You are allowed to run over a red traffic light at an intersection under the following circumstances: If you want to carefully give way to an emergency vehicle with a blue flashing light and an emergency horn or if a police officer is regulating the traffic at the intersection.
answerASource: To carefully afford space to an emergency vehicle with a blue flashing light and siren sounding
answerBSource: If there is a police officer on the junction directing traffic
answerCSource: Under no circumstances

questionTextSource: You now want to turn right at these traffic lights with a green arrow sign. What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: If there is a green arrow sign at a traffic light, you are allowed to cross a red light if you have stopped at the stop line first. You may turn if there is no risk of obstructing or endangering other road users.
answerASource: I initially halt at the stop line
answerBSource: I ensure that other road users are not impeded
answerCSource: Without stopping, I take the turn at walking speed

questionTextSource: What is the rule at this traffic-light-controlled junction with additional signage?
explanationSource: On traffic lights with this additional sign, only cyclists, even at red, can turn to the right.
answerASource: - cyclists are permitted to turn right, even when the light is showing red
answerBSource: - motor vehicles are permitted to turn right, even when the light is showing red
answerCSource: - cyclists must turn right

questionTextSource: The traffic lights facing you have already been “green” for some time now. What is the right course of action now?
explanationSource: As the traffic light has been green for some time, there is a high probability that it will change to yellow at any moment. Keep driving, ready to brake, because you will have to stop if it switches to yellow. You must not speed up, as a maximum of 30 km/h is permitted.
answerASource: I continue driving, ready to brake because the traffic lights could change to “amber”
answerBSource: I watch the traffic lights closely
answerCSource: I accelerate to ensure that I can still safely pass through the junction while the traffic lights are “green”

questionTextSource: You want to turn left and the lights are "green". On your left there is a tram wanting to proceed straight ahead. What is correct?
explanationSource: Although you are allowed to drive, due to the green light, you have to grant the tram the right of way, as it continues straight ahead. You should therefore only enter the intersection halfway and let oncoming vehicles pass before turning left.
answerASource: You must wait
answerBSource: The tram must wait
answerCSource: You must come to an agreement since there are no rules for this

questionTextSource: You have taken the right lane by mistake although you want to turn left. How are you allowed to proceed?
explanationSource: Due to the solid line, you must not change the lane here. Follow only the arrows on your lane. Turning left from this lane would be prohibited and would endanger or at least hinder other drivers.
answerASource: Straight ahead
answerBSource: To the right
answerCSource: To the left when the traffic lights are "green"

questionTextSource: What is the right behaviour now?
explanationSource: Since this situation is a two-phase traffic light, there is no green signal. After the red light is off, you can catch up with the truck. If there was no space left behind the truck to the exit on the right, you would have to wait at the traffic lights until there was enough space to stop.
answerASource: - off now
answerBSource: - off only when the lorry drives on
answerCSource: - off only when my light is green

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action now?
explanationSource: The traffic lights are out of order, therefore the right-of-way traffic signs apply. You must give way.
answerASource: I allow any cross traffic the right of way
answerBSource: I wait at the stop line for the next set of green lights
answerCSource: I drive through the junction quickly, because "yellow" is displayed

questionTextSource: A vehicle with flashing blue light and siren is approaching. What do you do?
explanationSource: A blue flashing light and siren are used in emergency situations such as car accidents or house fires. For this reason, you have to clear a passage for the emergency vehicles as fast as you can. Therefore, slowly reduce your speed and drive to the right edge of the road. If necessary, drive on the sidewalk and stay there.
answerASource: Clear the way for the vehicle
answerBSource: Drive carefully onto the pavement if necessary
answerCSource: At all events, brake hard and stop

questionTextSource: You hear a siren. Which questions must you ask yourself?
explanationSource: If you hear a siren, you should first determine which direction the emergency vehicle is coming from. Then you have to consider where you can go, without hindering the emergency vehicle from continuing and without endangering yourself.
answerASource: Where is the emergency vehicle coming from?
answerBSource: Could I obstruct the emergency vehicle?
answerCSource: Is this vehicle allowed to use a siren at all?

questionTextSource: What can a flashing amber light on a vehicle be a warning for?
explanationSource: A yellow flashing light warns against dangers that require particularly careful driving behavior. These hazards are work or accident sites or unusually slow, wide or long vehicles.
answerASource: For dangers at roadworks or the scene of an accident
answerBSource: For an exceptionally wide vehicle
answerCSource: For a large transport proceeding at low speed

questionTextSource: What does it mean when the flashing blue light of a vehicle is switched on but not the siren?
explanationSource: A blue flashing light warns you of accidents or situations such as a fire run. Any driver should allow vehicles with blue flashing lights to pass through, as this may indicate an emergency, vehicle escort or a platoon.
answerASource: Warning of an accident or other dangers
answerBSource: Warning of fire-brigade in action
answerCSource: A breakdown vehicle is on duty and on its way to its destination

questionTextSource: What can a flashing blue light (without siren) mean?
explanationSource: A blue flashing light without siren indicates to accidents or other emergencies - so you should allow free passage here as well. Blue light is also used during blue light driving. It can also be used to secure platoons.
answerASource: The vehicle is on duty. You should give way
answerBSource: Advance warning of a closed convoy
answerCSource: Without a siren, a flashing blue light does not mean anything

questionTextSource: You are being followed by an ambulance with its blue lights flashing and siren sounding. What should you do?
explanationSource: You are required to make space for the ambulance as soon as possible. Be careful not to endanger other road users. If possible, turn right if you are on a right lane; on the highway and multi-lane roads an alley must be formed.
answerASource: I move out of the way immediately without endangering others
answerBSource: I prompt the driver of the vehicle beside me to make way
answerCSource: I make way immediately once the traffic light has turned green

questionTextSource: At which places where there are no traffic signs regulating priority does the rule "right before left" apply?
explanationSource: At intersections and T-junctions without a traffic-regulating traffic sign "left yields to right" applies. If a vehicle leaves a property or a restricted traffic area, all other road users, including pedestrians and children on the sidewalk, have priority.
answerASource: At crossroads and junctions
answerBSource: At driveways
answerCSource: At the end of a traffic calmed area

questionTextSource: At which places where there are no traffic signs regulating priority does the rule "right before left" apply?
explanationSource: At intersections and T-junctions without priority signs, the vehicles coming from the right have priority. The same applies to roads that are equally ranked according to their signage. Lanes with drop kerb, drives as well as field and forest trails are an exception. If you are on one of these roads, you must give priority to other road users.
answerASource: At crossroads and junctions
answerBSource: At junctions with sunken kerbstone
answerCSource: At junctions of farm tracks or forest tracks with other roads

questionTextSource: You are approaching a crossroads where the priority situation is not clear to you straightaway. What do you do?
explanationSource: There are situations that are difficult to understand in terms of the right of way. If this happens to you wait and watch other car drivers. If the situation remains difficult, you can communicate with each other by clear hand signals. To simply continue straight ahead or act according to the rule "left yields to right" would be too risky and accident-prone.
answerASource: Wait, observe and come to an agreement with others, if necessary
answerBSource: Proceed according to the rule "right before left"
answerCSource: Always proceed when driving straight ahead

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: It is necessary to comply to the left yields to right rule. Grant the motorcyclist from the right the right of way before entering the intersection. Next, you as a left-turning driver, have to give priority to the oncoming cyclist. For the motorcyclist the cyclist comes from the right and thus has priority.
answerASource: I have to allow the cyclist to pass through
answerBSource: I have to allow the [motorcyclist] to pass through
answerCSource: I may make the turn before the cyclist

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Left yields to right applies in intersections and T-junctions without a traffic sign. Since no vehicle comes from the right, you may first cross the intersection. After that the truck drives and finally the cyclist.
answerASource: I may be the first to pass through the junction
answerBSource: I have to allow the [red truck] to turn
answerCSource: I have to allow the [cyclist] to turn

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: The traffic sign "Give way" regulates the right of way here. That means you have to wait and let both, the right and left vehicle, pass.
answerASource: I have to allow the [blue truck] to pass through
answerBSource: I have to allow the [tractor] to turn
answerCSource: The [blue truck] has to allow me to pass through

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Here the traffic sign "right of way at the next intersection or T-junction" applies. Since you want to turn left you will have to let the oncoming vehicle pass. You may then turn in front of the vehicle coming from the right.
answerASource: I have to allow the [motorcycle] to pass through
answerBSource: I take priority before [the bus]
answerCSource: I have to allow [the bus] to pass through

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: The traffic sign "Give way" suspends the left yields to right regulation. Thus, the motorcycle coming from the left has priority. You have to wait. Then the left-turning driver of the oncoming traffic must first let you cross the intersection before he can turn.
answerASource: I have to allow [the motorcycle] to pass through
answerBSource: I take priority before the [bus]
answerCSource: I have to allow the [bus] to pass through

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: The traffic sign stands for a "priority road". You may therefore turn in front of the vehicle coming from the left. First, however, you must let the oncoming vehicle turn, because you have to wait as a left-turning driver.
answerASource: - only turn after the [motorcycle]
answerBSource: - turn before the [yellow car]
answerCSource: - be the first to make my turn

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource: The right-of-way rule says that you have to let the yellow vehicle drive through.
answerASource: I allow [the yellow vehicle] to pass through
answerBSource: I take the turn ahead of the [yellow vehicle]
answerCSource: I take the turn ahead of the [red] car

questionTextSource: What is the right thing to do?
explanationSource: Since there are no signs at this intersection, which determine the priority of the drivers, the right of way "left yields to right" applies. For this reason, you need to let the car on your right pass first. Afterwards, the driver on the left has to give you the right of way.
answerASource: I give way to the [green car]
answerBSource: I turn left before the [green car]
answerCSource: I have to allow the [red car] to pass

questionTextSource: What is the right thing to do?
explanationSource: According to the signage, the motorcyclist is on the priority road and is therefore allowed to drive first. Both the driver of the blue car and you are on a lane with the "Give way" sign. As this does not clear up the situation, "left yields to right" applies. For this reason, you have to let the other vehicle drive through.
answerASource: I have to give way to the motorcycle
answerBSource: I have to give way to the [blue car]
answerCSource: I may drive out before the [blue car]

questionTextSource: Who has priority here?
explanationSource: The electric scooter follows the priority road and therefore has the right of way.
answerASource: The electric scooter
answerBSource: [The red truck]
answerCSource: Me

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource: The green and red cars are on a turning main road. You are on the secondary road. The green car remains on the main road, which means that the green car is allowed to drive first, then the red car and you are allowed to drive last.
answerASource: The [green] car may proceed ahead of the [red] car
answerBSource: I may proceed ahead of the [red] car
answerCSource: I may proceed ahead of the [green] car

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource: You and the yellow car are on a turning main road. The red car is on a secondary road.  Because the yellow car remains on the main road, it is allowed to drive first. You may drive second and the red car can drive last.
answerASource: I may proceed ahead of the [red car]
answerBSource: [The yellow car] may proceed ahead of the [red car]
answerCSource: I may proceed ahead of the [yellow car]

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: The sign at the intersection indicates that you must give way. Since you want to turn left, you must let the tram as well as the oncoming motorbike pass and enter the intersection last.
answerASource: I have to allow the tram to pass through
answerBSource: I have to allow the [motorcycle] to pass through
answerCSource: The tram must allow me to make the turn first

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource:
The motorbike is on a turning main road and is allowed to turn in front of the yellow car and you.
According to the "right before left" rule at crossroads, you may drive in front of the yellow car.
answerASource: The motorbike may proceed ahead of the [yellow car]
answerBSource: I may proceed ahead of the [yellow car]
answerCSource: I may proceed ahead of the [motorbike]

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource:
At this intersection applies to the right before left. So you can drive first.
"Right before left" applies to intersections where the right of way is not regulated by signs, traffic lights or traffic police officers.
answerASource: I may be the first to proceed
answerBSource: I have to allow [the red car] to turn
answerCSource: I have to allow [the cyclist] to pass through

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource:
At this junction, the right before left rule applies, which means that you must let the yellow vehicle pass.
You must also let the lorry through, as you want to turn left and the lorry wants to continue straight ahead in its lane.
answerASource: I have to allow the [yellow car] to pass through
answerBSource: I have to allow the [blue truck] to pass through
answerCSource: I may proceed before the [yellow car]

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: You are on the turning priority road and follow its course. Therefore, you must drive first and the other vehicle must wait.
answerASource: I may drive through
answerBSource: I have to allow [the blue car] to pass through

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource: You are approaching a priority road that is about to turn. As you are coming from a side road, you must allow the blue car leaving the priority road to pass. You must also stop at a stop sign at the stop line before you are allowed to enter the junction.
answerASource: - allow [the blue car] to pass through
answerBSource: - stop on the stop line
answerCSource: - allow [the red car] to pass through

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: At an intersection, which has no traffic signs, "left yields to right" applies. If a car drives from the right wants to turn right, he is allowed to drive first and you have to wait. On the other hand, if a vehicle approaching you wants to turn left, you have the right of way, because if a driver wants to turn, must always give priority to oncoming traffic.
answerASource: I allow [the blue car] to turn
answerBSource: I am the first to proceed
answerCSource: I allow the motorcycle to turn before me

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: The traffic sign indicates that you are on a priority road. The tram and the driver of the red car, on the other hand, must follow the sign "Give way" and wait. You drive first.
answerASource: I may be the first to proceed
answerBSource: I have to give way to the [red car]
answerCSource: I have to give way to the tram

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: If you are traveling on a priority road, you are always allowed to drive first. A car that meets you and wants to turn left, however, has to wait. If a car driver wants to turn into your street from the right, he/she must also give you the right of way, since you are on a priority road.
answerASource: I may drive through
answerBSource: I have to allow right of way to [the blue car]
answerCSource: I have to allow right of way to [the green car]

questionTextSource: What is the correct conduct?
explanationSource:
The following hierarchy applies on a priority road: In principle, vehicles on a priority road always have priority. However, when leaving a priority road, you must give priority to oncoming vehicles. However, vehicles that want to drive onto a priority road must give priority to other road users on the priority road.
This means you have to give priority to the tram because it is traveling on a priority road, does not change direction and you want to turn left.
answerASource: I let the tram through
answerBSource: I let [the tractor] turn
answerCSource: I drive first

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Due to the rule "left yields to right" you would have priority over the green car. However, you must give priority to the oncoming cyclist whose lane you would be crossing. To ensure the traffic flow, it is therefore advisable to let the driver of the green car first enter the intersection.
answerASource: I have to allow the cyclist to pass through
answerBSource: I forgo my right of way so as to not block the junction
answerCSource: I may turn left before the cyclist

questionTextSource: You want to turn right into a priority road. What must you look out for?
explanationSource: If you turn right into a priority road, you must give priority to all road users. This applies both to vehicles that come from the right and those that pass from the left. In addition, be sure to take care of cyclists and pedestrians who are to your right or behind you.
answerASource: Vehicles coming from the left and from the right
answerBSource: For cyclists and pedestrians alongside you to the right or behind you
answerCSource: Only for vehicles coming from the left

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Since the right of way at this intersection is not determined by appropriate signage, the principle "left yields to right" applies. Thus, you must give priority to the yellow car coming from the right. The blue truck also has priority over you, as you would cross the lane as a left-turning driver.
answerASource: - after the [blue truck]
answerBSource: - after the [yellow car]
answerCSource: - before the [yellow car]

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: At this intersection you have right of way. This is indicated by the traffic sign. That's why the vehicles have to wait from the right and left and let you drive first.
answerASource: - before [the two cars]
answerBSource: - after [the two cars]
answerCSource: - after [the blue car]

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: If you want to turn left from a priority road and another car is driving next to you, which remains on the priority road, you must give priority to this car driver. A vehicle that is not on the priority road, on the other hand, has to wait.
answerASource: I have to allow [the red car] to pass through
answerBSource: I may proceed before [the blue truck]
answerCSource: I may only proceed after [the blue truck]

questionTextSource: What is correct in this situation?
explanationSource: You are on a turning priority road, but you don't want to follow it to the right and instead continue straight ahead. From the right a motorcycle follows the course of the priority road. You have to wait and give priority to this vehicle.
answerASource: I have to allow [the motorbike] the right of way
answerBSource: [The motorbike] has to wait
answerCSource: I have to signal a left-turn

questionTextSource: Who has priority?
explanationSource: You follow a turning priority road. Therefore, you have priority over the driver of the car driving straight ahead. The driver of the tractor also has to wait because he/she leaves the priority road and the rule left yields to right applies to you.
answerASource: Me
answerBSource: [The red car]
answerCSource: [The green tractor]

questionTextSource: What is the right thing to do?
explanationSource: If no traffic signs have been erected to regulate the right of way, this is so-called circular traffic. Here, the basic rule of right before left applies. As you are turning into circular traffic, you must signal.
answerASource: I have to indicate
answerBSource: The [yellow] car has to wait
answerCSource: I have to wait

questionTextSource: Who is required to wait?
explanationSource: If a vehicle wants to turn from a field or forest path into a road, the car driver in question must wait. The rule "left yields to right" does not apply here.
answerASource: [The car that] is exiting the dirt track
answerBSource: I have to wait

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Who comes from the right, has right of way at intersections and T-junctions. Because if there is no priority-controlling traffic sign, "left yields to right" applies. No matter the size and type of the vehicle.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: [The motorbike] is obliged to wait

questionTextSource: You want to turn left. Who is required to wait?
explanationSource: At intersections and T-junctions without a traffic-regulating traffic sign, the rule "left yields to right" applies. Field and forest paths, however, are subordinate traffic routes. If you want to turn from a field or forest path onto a road, you have to wait.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: The blue vehicle has to wait

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: If you want to turn into a roundabout, you will be asked to wait by the "Give way" traffic sign. The drivers driving in a circle have priority here.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: The cyclist has to wait

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Here, "left yields to right" applies as well as the rules for left turns. The car coming from the right may drive first. Since you want to turn left, the oncoming cyclist has right of way.
answerASource: I have to allow the cyclist to turn
answerBSource: I have to allow [the blue car] to pass through
answerCSource: [The blue car] has to allow me to turn

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: If a road user comes from the right, you must pay attention to the "left yields to right" rule and wait. This also applies if it is a cyclist.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: The cyclist has to wait
answerCSource: I have to wait at the line of sight

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: At this spot, the right of way is not regulated by traffic signs. Therefore, at this intersection left yields to right applies. Here the straight driving cyclist has priority over you as you want to turn. The truck coming from the left may drive last.
answerASource: I have to allow the cyclist to pass through
answerBSource: I may make the turn in front of the cyclist
answerCSource: The blue truck may proceed first

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: A vehicle coming from the left is already on the priority road. Therefore, it is allowed to drive first. If you turn left, you will have to let oncoming vehicles drive past first. Due to the traffic sign, you have to stop at the stop line ("Give way") or stop completely and are not even allowed to trundle ("Stop sign").
answerASource: - give way to [the cyclist]
answerBSource: - give way to the [bus]
answerCSource: - stop on the stop line

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Since the right of way here is not regulated by traffic signs, left yields to right applies. This means: you must stop and grant access to vehicles from the right. The bike coming from the left has to let you in.
answerASource: I have to allow [the cyclist] to pass through
answerBSource: I may proceed before the [motorcycle]
answerCSource: [The cyclist] has to allow me to pass

questionTextSource: You would like to cross over a priority road. The view to both sides is very restricted due to parked vehicles. What should you do?
explanationSource: If visibility is limited you should only proceed with caution. Slowly get into the intersection until you can see the priority road. This is how you act responsibly.
answerASource: I carefully ease my way into the junction
answerBSource: I quickly cross over the junction
answerCSource: I sound my horn to warn crossing traffic

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: If you are on a priority road and you want to turn left, you will have to let oncoming cars drive by. If a vehicle drives next to you, which contrary to you remains on the priority road, then this car driver has priority. However, if a vehicle from another street with the traffic sign "Give way" wishes to turn into your priority road, he/she may only drive last.
answerASource: I have to allow [the blue vehicle] to pass
answerBSource: I may make the turn before [the green vehicle]
answerCSource: I have to allow [the green vehicle] to pass through

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: You are on the priority road. The tractor must therefore wait and let you turn left first. However, you must first let the tram that continues straight ahead on the priority road pass, since straight-ahead traffic always takes precedence over traffic that wants to turn.
answerASource: I have to allow the tram to pass through
answerBSource: I have to allow the tractor to pass through
answerCSource: I may not stop and wait on the rails

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: If you are at an intersection and you see the "Give way" traffic sign, you must wait and let the car driver pass through on the priority road.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: [The green car] has to wait

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: You and another road user are on a turning priority road. The other vehicle follows the course of the priority road, you want to leave. Therefore, you must let the other road user drive through first.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: [The yellow car] has to wait

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: You want to cross a turning priority road. Here you must give priority to all vehicles on the priority road and wait until the priority road is free for you to cross.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: I may pass through the junction before [the motorbike]

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: Without additional priority-regulating traffic signs or traffic lights, the general "left yields to right" rule applies. The car coming from the right must be given priority. You have priority over the car coming from the left.
answerASource: I have to allow right of way to the [red car]
answerBSource: I have right of way ahead of the [blue car]
answerCSource: I have to allow right of way to the [blue car]

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action?
explanationSource: At the traffic sign "right of way at the next intersection or T-junction", you may drive first at the next intersection. A vehicle coming from the left must wait.
answerASource: I move first
answerBSource: I have to allow [the yellow car] to pass through

questionTextSource: In what sequence may each continue driving?
explanationSource: You are on a turning priority road and you want to leave straight ahead. The cyclist follows the course of the priority road and therefore has priority over you. The car from the left must grant you right of way according to the rule "left yields to right".
answerASource: [The cyclist], me, [the yellow car]
answerBSource: [The cyclist], [the yellow car], me
answerCSource: Me, [the cyclist], [the yellow car]

questionTextSource: Who can move first in this situation?
explanationSource: The basic rule "right before left" does not apply to vehicles driving out of a field or forest path onto another road.
answerASource: Me
answerBSource: The [red] car
answerCSource: The [blue] tractor

questionTextSource: What is the right course of action here?
explanationSource: At intersections and junctions, whoever is coming from the right has the right of way.
answerASource: I wait until [the cyclist] has completed the turn
answerBSource: I take priority before [the cyclist]
answerCSource: I warn [the cyclist] by sounding the horn

questionTextSource: Which is correct in this situation?
explanationSource: You can see from the signs that this is not a roundabout. Therefore, right before left applies.
answerASource: I have to wait
answerBSource: [The blue car] has to wait
answerCSource: I have to reach an understanding with the driver of the [blue car]

questionTextSource: What do you do when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: This traffic sign alerts you about an approaching, confusing intersection or T-junction. You should slow down your speed and be ready to brake, since the right road user has priority here. You should also pay close attention to the traffic on the left, since you have to expect that it will not fulfill its obligation to wait and possibly ignore your right of way. Adjust your speed so that you can see the traffic coming from the right and, if necessary, brake without any problems.
answerASource: Reduce speed
answerBSource: Be ready to brake
answerCSource: Observe the traffic coming from the right only

questionTextSource: What should you remember when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: This traffic sign warns you of an incline of up to 10%. This means that you must be aware of the longer braking distance, which arises at this incline in contrast to a plane road. Since the brakes may become hot during extended braking and may lose function, it is important that the driver shifts to a lower gear to take advantage of the engine braking effect. Under no circumstances should you disengage.
answerASource: Braking distance is greater on downhill slopes than on level ground
answerBSource: The brakes may become less efficient with prolonged use
answerCSource: The brakes become more efficient when no gear is selected

questionTextSource: What do you do when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: Warning sign: It warns you of a narrowing of the lane in your direction of travel. If two lanes narrow to one, the zip merging takes effect. This means: there is no absolute priority; The driver of the narrowing lane must be able to merge seamlessly into the traffic.
answerASource: Give precedence to oncoming traffic
answerBSource: When there are two lanes in one direction, employ the zipper feed-in method
answerCSource: Where there are two lanes in one direction and you are driving on the left lane, you take absolute precedence

questionTextSource: You see this traffic sign. What must you reckon with?
explanationSource: If you see this traffic sign, you as a driver will have to expect on oncoming traffic and glare from headlights. It warns you where you would not normally expect oncoming traffic: when driving on one-way streets, along construction sites or at barriers of individual lanes on highways. For black arrows, no direction has priority.
answerASource: Oncoming traffic
answerBSource: Dazzle from oncoming traffic at night
answerCSource: Oncoming traffic taking precedence

questionTextSource: What does this traffic sign require of you?
explanationSource: The sign will point you to a school, kindergarten or playground nearby. Here you have to drive with adapted speed and increased attention. Children could suddenly run onto the road without paying attention to the traffic. Always be ready to brake.
answerASource: Greatest alertness
answerBSource: Readiness to brake
answerCSource: Reduction in speed

questionTextSource: What does this traffic sign combination indicate?
explanationSource: The exclamation mark indicates danger ahead. The sign with a rider shows that you have to expect horses and riders on the road.
answerASource: Danger in connection with horses
answerBSource: That people on horseback have priority over motor vehicles
answerCSource: No through-traffic because of a bridle-path

questionTextSource: What does this traffic sign combination indicate?
explanationSource: The exclamation mark stands for a general hazard warning. The exact nature of the hazard is shown by the additional sign: Here you must expect horse and cart on the road.
answerASource: Danger in connection with horse and carriages
answerBSource: That horse and carriages have priority over motor vehicles
answerCSource: Horse and carriages are not allowed to pass through

questionTextSource: What must you anticipate when you see this road sign?
explanationSource: This is a danger sign warning you of a pedestrian crossing. The pedestrian crossing may be difficult to see. Pedestrians will use the pedestrian crossing to cross the roadway. Be especially alert.
answerASource: A pedestrian crossing that is hard to see
answerBSource: Pedestrians that might cross the road
answerCSource: Pedestrians who may only cross the road from the right

questionTextSource: What must you do upon seeing this traffic sign?
explanationSource: This danger sign indicates that there is a pedestrian crossing in the immediate vicinity. You must watch out for pedestrians and let them cross the road. You should approach at a moderate speed, stop under certain circumstances and never overtake at the crossing.
answerASource: - drive up to the pedestrian crossing at a moderate speed
answerBSource: - be particularly attentive
answerCSource: - be ready to brake

questionTextSource: What danger is indicated to you by this traffic sign?
explanationSource: This traffic sign warns of strong crosswinds and that your vehicle could be displaced sideways.
answerASource: Sidewind
answerBSource: The risk that my vehicle may be shifted sideways
answerCSource: Air traffic

questionTextSource: You pass this traffic sign while driving outside a built-up area. How far off is the hazard to be expected?
explanationSource: The sign warns of danger ahead. Expect the dangerous area outside of built-up areas at a distance of 150 - 250 m. Reduce your speed and drive with increased attention.
answerASource: Between 150 m and 250 m
answerBSource: Between 50 m and 150 m
answerCSource: Between 250 m and 350 m

questionTextSource: What must you be prepared for when you pass these traffic signs?
explanationSource: You must be prepared for skiers and tobogganers crossing the road. This traffic sign informs you that on this road winter sports can be operated and no winter road clearance clears the snow. You need to expect increased danger of slipping due to the snow that sticks to the road.
answerASource: - this road may be used for winter sports
answerBSource: - there is packed snow or black ice on the roadway
answerCSource: - only the pavements are used for winter sports

questionTextSource: What do you do when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: The sign warns you of a double bend. First expect a right turn, then a left turn. You could drive onto the oncoming lane. Adjust your speed and drive far to the right to avoid collision with oncoming traffic.
answerASource: Reduce your speed
answerBSource: Drive as far as possible over on the right-hand side
answerCSource: Accelerate strongly immediately after the right bend

questionTextSource: What do you have to expect after these traffic signs?
explanationSource: After this traffic sign, an incline of 10% starts. The additional sign informs about the length of the danger zone of 800 meters. The arrows to the right and left indicate that the incline starts from the sign. Without arrows it would mean that the incline begins at a distance of 800 meters.
answerASource: A downhill slope 800 m in length
answerBSource: An uphill slope 800 m in length
answerCSource: The beginning of a downhill slope 800 m ahead

questionTextSource: What do you do when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: The traffic sign informs about a strong incline of 12%. Depending on the vehicle performance, it is necessary to shift down to a lower gear, so that you do not create a traffic obstruction and a danger to others due to driving too slowly. When you accelerate while going uphill and you don't get faster, you need to gear down.
answerASource: Change down gear, if necessary
answerBSource: Change to a higher gear in order to maintain your speed
answerCSource: Brake so that your speed will not increase too much on the downhill slope

questionTextSource: What should you do when you approach this traffic sign?
explanationSource: The road in front of you is uneven and may have diagonal or longitudinal grooves. This sign warns against them. You cannot only get into a skid in damp and cold weather. Avoid fast steering movements.
answerASource: Reduce your speed
answerBSource: Increase your speed
answerCSource: Avoid the uneven patches by making sudden steering wheel movements

questionTextSource: What dangers can arise if this traffic sign is ignored?
explanationSource: Due to inappropriate driving there is a risk of skidding on this section of the route with endangerment of oncoming traffic. The route is uneven with possible diagonal and longitudinal grooves. This means that your tires will have lower grip. You need to throttle your speed in order to be able to steer the vehicle safely.
answerASource: The vehicle could go into a skid and endanger oncoming traffic
answerBSource: The vehicle could suffer a broken axle or spring fracture
answerCSource: The load could be damaged

questionTextSource: What does this traffic sign warn you of?
explanationSource: The road in front of you has an increased risk of skidding when it's wet or dirty. You should reduce your speed and not brake or steer abruptly.
answerASource: Danger of skidding when the roadway is dirty
answerBSource: Danger of skidding when the roadway is wet
answerCSource: Drivers under the influence of alcohol

questionTextSource: What do you do when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: This is a warning sign and it warns drivers of an imminent narrowing of the road. You have to expect oncoming traffic and behave as follows: Reduce your speed and refrain from overtaking maneuvers. Stopping in front of the bottleneck is only required if an oncoming vehicle is already in the bottleneck. Instead of a specific priority, zip merging applies, according to which the drivers are merging into the lane.
answerASource: Reduce your speed
answerBSource: Refrain from overtaking
answerCSource: Always stop before the road narrows

questionTextSource: What must you reckon with when you see these traffic signs?
explanationSource: Expect a narrowed lane 50 m ahead. Please drive slowly and ready for braking to the danger area to be able to react to traffic congestion.
answerASource: The roadway narrowing approximately 50 m ahead
answerBSource: The roadway narrowing for 50 m
answerCSource: A road widening 50 m ahead

questionTextSource: What must you reckon with when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: When there's a construction site it is to be expected that construction site vehicles and workers can come very close to flowing traffic. Also building materials and tools could be on the road. Even if the construction site is not operated at the moment, a soiled road is to be expected. There is a risk of slipping and skidding.
answerASource: Traffic from road construction vehicles
answerBSource: Men at work on the roadway
answerCSource: Construction materials on the roadway

questionTextSource: What must you reckon with when you see this traffic sign?
explanationSource: This traffic sign warns against congestion on this section. Adjust your speed and the safety distance to the vehicle ahead according to the dangerous situation. The vehicle in front of you could suddenly brake. Also keep an eye on the traffic flow in front of the vehicle in front of you. You may drive a little offset within the lane.
answerASource: Traffic congestion
answerBSource: The vehicles driving in front may suddenly brake
answerCSource: The roadway widens into three lanes

questionTextSource: What does this traffic sign indicate?
explanationSource: Please pay attention to a badly recognizable light indicator like a traffic light. In the further course of the road a danger spot like an intersection or a construction site area might possibly follow.
answerASource: Traffic lights
answerBSource: A customs post
answerCSource: A level crossing with flashing red light

questionTextSource: What do these traffic signs indicate?
explanationSource: Pay attention to an unguarded level crossing, if you drive to the right at this point or turn. If you continue straight ahead, this sign has no meaning for you.
answerASource: A level crossing on the road turning right
answerBSource: The direction from which a train could come
answerCSource: A diversion avoiding the level crossing

questionTextSource: What does this traffic sign indicate?
explanationSource: The sign that warns you of a level crossing ahead informs you about a level crossing that is 160 meters away. Approach the danger zone cautiously and ready to brake in case a train crosses your path.
answerASource: A level crossing approximately 160 m ahead
answerBSource: A level crossing approximately 80 m ahead
answerCSource: A level crossing approximately 240 m ahead
